ComplexMap 1.1.2
  • Reference
    • 1. Preparing Your Input Data
    • 2. Start a Typical Workflow
    • 3. Advanced Analysis and Visualization
    • 4. Visualizing Quantitative Data
  • Changelog

3. Advanced Analysis and Visualization

Qingzhou Zhang

2026-02-11

Source: vignettes/vignette_03-advanced-analysis.Rmd
vignette_03-advanced-analysis.Rmd

Advanced Analysis Workflow

Once you have a ComplexMap object, the package provides several functions for deeper analysis and interpretation of the network structure. This vignette covers:

  1. Optimizing Parameters: Using evaluateComplexes() to tune the refineComplexList step.
  2. Summarizing & Querying Themes: A streamlined workflow using summarizeThemes() and queryMap() to identify and investigate biological clusters.
library(ComplexMap)
library(dplyr)

1. Optimizing Refinement with evaluateComplexes

The mergeThreshold in refineComplexList determines how aggressively redundant complexes are merged. The default is a conservative Jaccard similarity of 0.90 to preserve biological variants.

The evaluateComplexes() function can score a refined complex list against a gold standard, helping you tune this parameter.

# Load the package's demo and reference data
data(demoComplexes)
data(referenceComplexes)

# 1. Run Refinement
refinement_output <- refineComplexList(
  demoComplexes,
  mergeThreshold = 0.90,
  similarityMethod = "jaccard",
  verbose = FALSE
)

# 2. Evaluate
# Pass the $refinedComplexes element to the evaluation function
metrics <- evaluateComplexes(
  predictedComplexes = refinement_output$refinedComplexes,
  referenceComplexes = referenceComplexes,
  nCores = 2, # Use multiple cores if available
  verbose = FALSE
)

# Display the resulting metrics
knitr::kable(as.data.frame(metrics))
PPV Sn Acc MMR
0.8005051 0.2510583 0.4483006 0.0940346

For more systematic tuning, see the documentation for benchmarkParameters(), which automates this process over a range of thresholds.

2. A Streamlined Workflow for Theme Analysis

A key goal of ComplexMap is to organize complexes into a functional landscape. summarizeThemes() uses community detection to find densely connected physical neighborhoods (themes) and, by default, adds this information directly to your ComplexMap object. This makes querying by theme simple and intuitive.

First, let’s generate a map object for this example.

gmtPath <- getExampleGmt()
gmt <- getGmtFromFile(gmtPath, verbose = FALSE)

cm_obj_adv <- createComplexMap(
  complexList = demoComplexes,
  gmt = gmt,
  mergeThreshold = 0.85, 
  verbose = FALSE
)

Step 2.1: Summarize Themes and Add to Object

Now, we run summarizeThemes(). The default add_to_object = TRUE returns the modified ComplexMap object, now containing theme information in its node table.

# Ensure reproducibility of community detection
set.seed(123)

# This single command finds themes AND adds them to the object
cm_obj_adv <- summarizeThemes(cm_obj_adv, verbose = FALSE)

# We can inspect the new columns in the node table
getNodeTable(cm_obj_adv) %>%
  select(complexId, primaryFunctionalDomain, themeId, themeLabel) %>%
  head() %>%
  knitr::kable()
complexId primaryFunctionalDomain themeId themeLabel
C_17 BIOCARTA_CDC42RAC_PATHWAY 1 BIOCARTA_EEA1_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PTDINS_PATHWAY
C_527 BIOCARTA_CIRCADIAN_PATHWAY 2 BIOCARTA_CPSF_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_LIS1_PATHWAY
C_472 BIOCARTA_AGPCR_PATHWAY 1 BIOCARTA_EEA1_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PTDINS_PATHWAY
C_621 Unenriched 3 BIOCARTA_SUMO_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PARKIN_PATHWAY
C_514 BIOCARTA_RNA_PATHWAY 4 BIOCARTA_DNAFRAGMENT_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_CD40_PATHWAY
C_619 BIOCARTA_SAM68_PATHWAY 3 BIOCARTA_SUMO_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PARKIN_PATHWAY

The returned object is now “theme-aware”.

Step 2.2: Querying the Map for Specific Information

The queryMap() function can now be used with type = "theme" to easily retrieve all complexes belonging to a specific biological theme.

Query by Protein

Find all complexes that contain a specific protein.

# Find all complexes containing the protein "UBA1"
queryMap(cm_obj_adv, query = "UBA1", type = "protein") %>%
  select(complexId, primaryFunctionalDomain, proteinCount, themeLabel, proteins) %>%
  knitr::kable()
complexId primaryFunctionalDomain proteinCount themeLabel proteins
C_610 BIOCARTA_PROTEASOME_PATHWAY 18 BIOCARTA_PROTEASOME_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_ERAD_PATHWAY UBA1,TRMT1,RARS1,QARS1,NARS1,MARS1,LARS1,KARS1,IARS1,GARS1,EPRS1,DARS1,AARS1,VBP1,EEF1E1,ASNS,AIMP2,AIMP1
C_308 BIOCARTA_PROTEASOME_PATHWAY 4 BIOCARTA_PROTEASOME_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_ERAD_PATHWAY WDR73,USH1C,UBA1,NMD3

Query by Complex ID

Retrieve the data for a single complex.

# Get the data for complex "CpxMap_0001"
queryMap(cm_obj_adv, query = "CpxMap_0001", type = "complex") %>%
  # Use glimpse to see all columns, including theme info
  glimpse()
#> Rows: 0
#> Columns: 14
#> $ complexId               <chr> 
#> $ proteinCount            <int> 
#> $ proteins                <chr> 
#> $ primaryFunctionalDomain <chr> 
#> $ topEnrichedFunctions    <chr> 
#> $ colorHex                <chr> 
#> $ sizeMapping             <dbl> 
#> $ x                       <dbl> 
#> $ y                       <dbl> 
#> $ betweenness             <dbl> 
#> $ degree                  <dbl> 
#> $ themeId                 <int> 
#> $ themeLabel              <chr> 
#> $ themePurity             <dbl>

Query by Theme

Because we added theme labels to the object, we can now query them directly.

# First, let's find a valid theme label to query from our map
node_table <- getNodeTable(cm_obj_adv)
if (nrow(node_table) > 0 && "themeLabel" %in% names(node_table)) {
  target_theme <- na.omit(node_table$themeLabel)

  cat("Querying for all nodes in theme:", target_theme, "\n\n")

  # Query the map using the theme label
  theme_nodes <- queryMap(cm_obj_adv, query = target_theme, type = "theme")
  
  theme_nodes %>%
    select(complexId, primaryFunctionalDomain, themeLabel) %>%
    head() %>%
    knitr::kable()
}
#> Querying for all nodes in theme: BIOCARTA_EEA1_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PTDINS_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_CPSF_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_LIS1_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_EEA1_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PTDINS_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_SUMO_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PARKIN_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_DNAFRAGMENT_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_CD40_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_SUMO_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PARKIN_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_KREB_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_MALATEX_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_SUMO_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PARKIN_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_KREB_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_MALATEX_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_CPSF_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_LIS1_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_TID_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_CARM_ER_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_CPSF_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_LIS1_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_EEA1_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PTDINS_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_EEA1_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PTDINS_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_CELL2CELL_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_TEL_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_SM_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_IRES_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_MCM_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_EEA1_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PTDINS_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_RANMS_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_NPC_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_CPSF_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_LIS1_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_DNAFRAGMENT_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_CD40_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_TID_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_CARM_ER_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_BTG2_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_EPO_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_DNAFRAGMENT_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_CD40_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_KREB_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_MALATEX_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_BTG2_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_EPO_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_DNAFRAGMENT_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_CD40_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_DNAFRAGMENT_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_CD40_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_SM_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_RANMS_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_NPC_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_CELL2CELL_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_TEL_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_SM_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_KREB_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_MALATEX_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_CELL2CELL_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_TEL_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_EEA1_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PTDINS_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_CELL2CELL_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_TEL_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_S1P_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_MITOCHONDRIA_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_PROTEASOME_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_ERAD_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_RANMS_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_NPC_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_BTG2_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_EPO_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_EEA1_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_PTDINS_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_MTA3_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_EPONFKB_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_FREE_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_RANMS_PATHWAY / BIOCARTA_NPC_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_MRP_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_MPR_PATHWAY BIOCARTA_BTG2_PATHWAY /
